notes.anchor: the day before the Quinquatria there was a magnificent storm-wind
notes.note: The Quinquatrus, Minerva's festival, opened on 19 March; the eve gives Cicero a fixed date (18 March) for the gale he supposes carried Pompey's fleet out of Brundisium.
Caesar's nine years of conquest in Gaul (58--50 BC) had given him a hardened army and large levies of Gallic auxiliary cavalry and infantry, the military base from which he now overwhelmed Italy.

Cicero quotes Plato (Epistles 329d) on the menace lurking in a tyrant's "requests" --- pleas that are really commands, mixed with compulsion; he applies it to Caesar's coaxing invitations, which he fears more than open force.

Lanuvium was a hill town on the Via Appia south of Rome, in fashionable villa country; Cicero is house-hunting (for his brother Quintus) even amid the crisis, but has confused this property with another.

At Corfinium in February 49 BC Caesar had taken and then pointedly released the Pompeian garrison and its commanders; Lentulus Spinther, one of those freed, fears being put through the same humiliating capture-and-pardon again.

notes.anchor: the little booklet that has been thrown in with the letter
notes.note: Cicero often enclosed ("thrown in") with his own letters copies of the correspondence reaching him, here a libellus bundling the alarming despatches he has been receiving so Atticus can read the raw reports himself.
